icd_category: I21.0 — ST elevation myocardial infarction involving left anterior descending coronary artery
one_liner: A 58-year-old male with hypertension and hyperlipidemia presents with crushing substernal chest pain radiating to the left arm.

stage2.hpi_extension: Patient reports sudden onset of severe, crushing substernal chest pain beginning 45 minutes ago while mowing the lawn. Pain radiates to the left arm and jaw. Associated with diaphoresis, nausea, and shortness of breath. He took one aspirin at home before calling 911. Rates pain 9/10. No relief with rest. Denies recent illness, trauma, or similar episodes in the past.
stage2.pmh: Hypertension (10 years), hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes mellitus, former smoker (30 pack-years, quit 2 years ago)
stage2.medications: Lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily
stage2.family_history: Father died of MI at age 55. Mother has hypertension. Brother had CABG at age 60.
stage2.social_history: Former smoker (quit 2 years ago, 30 pack-year history). Drinks 2-3 beers on weekends. Works as a construction foreman. Married with two children.
stage2.physical_exam: General: **Diaphoretic, anxious male in moderate distress clutching his chest**. HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic; **Pupils equal, round, reactive**; Moist mucous membranes. Cardiovascular: **Tachycardic, regular rhythm**; **S1/S2 present**; **S4 gallop noted**; No murmurs. Pulmonary: Bibasilar crackles, no wheezing. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended; **Normoactive bowel sounds**. Extremities: No edema; **Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally**. Skin: **Cool, diaphoretic**. Neuro: Alert, oriented x4; No focal deficits.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated, consistent with this condition injury
Elevated, suggests myocardial stress and early heart failure
Elevated, likely stress hyperglycemia in known diabetic
Normal, important for arrhythmia risk assessment

imaging_results.result: ST elevation in leads V1-V4 (2-4 mm), reciprocal ST depression in leads II, III, aVF
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Diagnostic of anterior this condition involving LAD territory

imaging_results.result: Mild pulmonary vascular congestion. No pneumothorax. Normal cardiac silhouette.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Early pulmonary congestion consistent with LV dysfunction

imaging_results.result: Anterior wall hypokinesis. Estimated EF 35-40%. No pericardial effusion.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Regional wall motion abnormality confirms ischemic territory

stage5.final_clue: The ECG shows ST elevation in V1-V4 with reciprocal changes, and troponin is markedly elevated at 2.8 ng/mL.

debrief.classic_presentation: A middle-aged male with cardiac risk factors presenting with sudden-onset crushing substernal chest pain radiating to the left arm, associated with diaphoresis, nausea, and dyspnea. ECG shows ST elevation in contiguous leads with reciprocal changes, and cardiac biomarkers are elevated.
debrief.pathophysiology: STEMI results from complete occlusion of a coronary artery, usually due to rupture of an atherosclerotic plaque with subsequent thrombus formation. The resulting transmural ischemia causes ST elevation on ECG in the territory supplied by the occluded artery. Without reperfusion, irreversible myocardial necrosis begins within 20-40 minutes and progresses over hours.
debrief.treatment_overview: Immediate management: Aspirin 325 mg, P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel, ticagrelor, or prasugrel), heparin, and nitroglycerin (if no contraindications). Emergent reperfusion is critical: primary PCI (preferred if door-to-balloon <90 min) or fibrinolysis (if PCI not available within 120 min). Beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, and high-intensity statins should be initiated. Monitor for arrhythmias, heart failure, and mechanical complications.
high_yield_pearls: Door-to-balloon time goal is <90 minutes for primary PCI; door-to-needle <30 minutes for fibrinolysis.
Anterior STEMI (LAD occlusion) carries the highest mortality risk among STEMI locations.
Right-sided ECG (V4R) should be obtained if inferior STEMI is suspected to evaluate for RV involvement.
New left bundle branch block (LBBB) in the setting of chest pain should be treated as a STEMI equivalent.
Complications include cardiogenic shock, ventricular arrhythmias, free wall rupture, VSD, and papillary muscle rupture.
sources: Tintinalli's Emergency Medicine, 9th Edition, Ch. 49 — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search
2023 ESC Guidelines for Acute Coronary Syndromes — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/
